vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="80" width="150" x="-1086.32" y="-253.97000000000003" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="3YcymwowWl2byjLGE4et-5" parent="1" style="text;html=1;strokeColor=none;fillColor=none;align=center;verticalAlign=middle;whiteSpace=wrap;rounded=0;" value="Design pattern that involves an object (the provider) that supplies<br> dependencies or services to other objects (the consumers). This pattern<br> is useful for dependency injection, allowing for loose coupling." vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="30" width="206.77" x="-1114.7" y="-145.97000000000003" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="vnjzez0KHtbDO86xciZE-1" parent="1" style="text;html=1;strokeColor=default;fillColor=default;spacing=5;spacingTop=-20;whiteSpace=wrap;overflow=hidden;rounded=0;" value="<h2><font style="font-size: 24px;">How do Application Logic and Business Logic Work Together?</font></h2><h1></h1><p align="justify">Even though they each have distinct functions, business logic and <br>application logic work together to ensure that a business runs smoothly <br>and efficiently. Companies rely on both types of logic to automate <br>tasks, keep data safe, and provide a consistent user experience.&nbsp;</p><p align="justify">The two types of code are often combined within an application or program. For example, an e-commerce application might have business logic that defines the process for adding items to a shopping cart and application logic that actually adds the items to the cart.</p><p align="justify">When an&nbsp; application needs to perform a task, it uses <b>business logic</b> <b>to <br>determine how to carry it out</b>. <b>The business logic will tell the <br>application what rules to follow and in what order they should be <br>performed</b>. The application logic will then use this information to carry out those steps.</p><p align="justify">The important thing to remember is that business logic and <br>application logic need to work together in order for a company to be <br>successful. They are essential for creating a successful web application<br> that is both efficient and user-friendly.<br><br><i><font style="font-size: 10px;">This was taken from https://www.apisec.ai/blog/business-logic-vs-application-logic</font></i><br></p><p></p>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="400" width="398" x="-693.4400000000003" y="-1440" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> h2.font: How do Application Logic and Business Logic Work Together?

div.p:
Even though they each have distinct functions, business logic and
application logic work together to ensure that a business runs smoothly
and efficiently. Companies rely on both types of logic to automate
tasks, keep data safe, and provide a consistent user experience.

The two types of code are often combined within an application or program. For example, an e-commerce application might have business logic that defines the process for adding items to a shopping cart and application logic that actually adds the items to the cart.

When an application needs to perform a task, it uses business logic to
determine how to carry it out. The business logic will tell the
application what rules to follow and in what order they should be
performed. The application logic will then use this information to carry out those steps.

The important thing to remember is that business logic and
application logic need to work together in order for a company to be
successful. They are essential for creating a successful web application
that is both efficient and user-friendly.
This was taken from https://www.apisec.ai/blog/business-logic-vs-application-logic

div.div: Functions used to access or "select" specific pieces of the state from the global state object. They provide a convenient way to extract and format the necessary state data, often optimizing the performance by memoizing results. It will contain functions that retrieve particular pieces of state for use throughout the app.
